title: The Twisted World Of Marge Simpson

jokes.text: D'oh! [Screams]
jokes.setup: Homer crashes his car in the opening sequence
jokes.punchline: Classic Homer exclamation followed by impact scream
jokes.explanation: Iconic Homer catchphrase, perfect character integration but relies on established formula

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: D'oh!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Woo-hoo!

jokes.text: our shares in Dynaflux Unimatics... are up a delicious seven and three-quarters
jokes.setup: Investment club discussing stock performance
jokes.punchline: Using 'delicious' to describe stock gains, treating money like food
jokes.explanation: Mild wordplay connecting food and finance themes

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Well, our portfolio is up 3% this quarter.

dialogue.speaker: Helen
dialogue.line: Mmm, delicious gains.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Helen, it's just money.

dialogue.speaker: Helen
dialogue.line: Mmm, delicious money.

jokes.text: Children are so fat today. Isn't there some way we can make money off that?
jokes.setup: Discussing investment opportunities
jokes.punchline: Coldly calculating how to profit from childhood obesity
jokes.explanation: Sharp dark humor with excellent economy, perfectly captures character's ruthless nature

dialogue.speaker: Agnes Skinner
dialogue.line: Coldly calculating how to profit from childhood obesity

jokes.text: Tsk. Oh, Marge. You are such a wet blanket.
jokes.setup: Marge expresses discomfort with risky investments
jokes.punchline: Classic condescending dismissal
jokes.explanation: Standard insult, fits character but lacks originality

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: I'm not comfortable with such risky investments.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Oh Marge, don't worry your pretty little head about it.

jokes.text: If we'd listened to you, we wouldn't have sponsored that Mexican wrestler.
jokes.setup: Criticizing Marge's conservative investment approach
jokes.punchline: Reveals they actually sponsored a Mexican wrestler as an investment
jokes.explanation: Wonderfully absurd detail that suggests their 'sophisticated' investment club makes bizarre choices

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: I think we should be more conservative with our investments.

dialogue.speaker: Helen
dialogue.line: Conservative? Marge, we actually sponsored a Mexican wrestler as an investment!

jokes.text: Well, Marge. You're about as popular as rug burn. [Chuckles]
jokes.setup: Agnes delivering final insult to Marge
jokes.punchline: Bizarre comparison to rug burn with self-satisfied chuckle
jokes.explanation: Perfectly captures Agnes's mean-spirited nature with an oddly specific insult

dialogue.speaker: Agnes
dialogue.line: Your hair looks like it was styled by someone who got rug burn and kept going!

jokes.text: You'll get your pancakes in the mail.
jokes.setup: Marge leaving the investment club meeting
jokes.punchline: Absurd threat to mail pancakes as petty revenge
jokes.explanation: Brilliantly absurd Marge moment - passive-aggressive but completely illogical

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: That's it! I'm mailing you all pancakes!

jokes.text: When are the pancakes coming in the mail?
jokes.setup: Marge explains being kicked out of investment club
jokes.punchline: Homer focuses on the least important detail - the pancakes
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer character moment - completely missing the point while fixated on food

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: They kicked me out of the investment club.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: They kicked you out? But what about the pancakes?

jokes.text: Oh! Call work and tell 'em I won't be in tomorrow. Ow!
jokes.setup: Homer confidently declaring his earning power protects the family
jokes.punchline: Immediately hurts himself and needs time off work
jokes.explanation: Classic setup/payoff structure undermining Homer's confidence with immediate irony

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: My earning power protects this family!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Ow! Ow! Ow!

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Homer, what happened?

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: I'm gonna need some time off work.

jokes.text: Greetings, humans. I am Investo the Robot of the planet Opportuniac.
jokes.setup: Robot greeting at franchise fair
jokes.punchline: Ridiculous name 'Investo' from planet 'Opportuniac'
jokes.explanation: Standard sci-fi parody with obvious puns

dialogue.speaker: Investo Robot
dialogue.line: Greetings! I am Investo from the planet Opportuniac.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Investo? What kind of ridiculous name is that?

dialogue.speaker: Investo Robot
dialogue.line: It is the custom of my people.

jokes.text: We're trying to, but you're blocking our way. Danger! Danger! Don't forget to pick up pamphlets.
jokes.setup: Robot blocking entrance while telling them to enter
jokes.punchline: Robot's contradictory behavior and mundane concern about pamphlets
jokes.explanation: Mild robot malfunction humor

dialogue.speaker: Investo Robot
dialogue.line: You must enter!

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: But you're blocking the door.

dialogue.speaker: Investo Robot
dialogue.line: Yes, well, please try not to bend the pamphlets.

jokes.text: I'll handle this. [Sizzling, Clunking, Beeping] Help, help, help. Security code 30.
jokes.setup: Homer decides to deal with malfunctioning robot
jokes.punchline: Homer apparently electrocutes the robot, causing it to malfunction worse
jokes.explanation: Classic Homer 'solution' making things worse

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: That'll teach you to malfunction!

dialogue.speaker: Investo Robot
dialogue.line: BZZZZT! WARNING! WARNING! CRITICAL SYSTEMS FAILURE!

jokes.text: And while you're there, would you pick up some of that nice green moon money for me- No deal, McCutcheon. That moon money is mine!
jokes.setup: Salesman using rocket metaphor
jokes.punchline: Salesman seriously discussing fictional moon money as if it's real
jokes.explanation: Absurd escalation of sales metaphor into actual belief

dialogue.speaker: Franchise Salesman
dialogue.line: Your investment will skyrocket! We're talking exponential growth, stratospheric returns!

dialogue.speaker: Royce McCutcheon
dialogue.line: How much are we talking here?

dialogue.speaker: Franchise Salesman
dialogue.line: Well, initial investment is $50,000. But within five years, you could be making $500,000 a year. And that's just in U.S. dollars. On the moon, that's worth twice as much.

dialogue.speaker: Royce McCutcheon
dialogue.line: On the moon?

dialogue.speaker: Franchise Salesman
dialogue.line: Lunar credits. They're very stable. The moon colony economy has been booming.

jokes.text: scientists say 40% of America's pictures are hanging crooked
jokes.setup: Salesman presenting a business opportunity
jokes.punchline: Completely made-up statistic about crooked pictures
jokes.explanation: Satirizes how salespeople invent problems with fake statistics

dialogue.speaker: Frame Nudging Salesman
dialogue.line: Did you know that 4 out of 5 homes have at least one crooked picture frame? That's a $47 billion untapped market!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Wow, that's terrible!

dialogue.speaker: Frame Nudging Salesman
dialogue.line: Terrible? It's an opportunity! With Frame Nudging, you could be making money hand over fist!

jokes.text: Those fat cats in Washington? [Laughs] Good luck.
jokes.setup: Who will fix all the crooked pictures?
jokes.punchline: Treating picture straightening as if it requires government intervention
jokes.explanation: Escalates the absurdity by involving politics in picture hanging

dialogue.speaker: Homer Simpson
dialogue.line: We need the government to fix all these crooked pictures!

dialogue.speaker: Lenny
dialogue.line: Yeah, this is a job for the Department of Picture Straightening.

dialogue.speaker: Carl
dialogue.line: I'm calling my congressman!

jokes.text: including, 'Who are you?' and 'What are you doing here?'
jokes.setup: Booklet of commonly asked questions
jokes.punchline: Questions reveal customers will be confused and suspicious
jokes.explanation: Excellent dark implication that the business model is fundamentally flawed

dialogue.speaker: Frame Nudging Salesman
dialogue.line: Here's our booklet of commonly asked questions.

dialogue.speaker: Frame Nudging Salesman
dialogue.line: As you can see, our customers will be very confused and suspicious.

jokes.text: Oh, my name is Agnes, and you know it's Agnes! It means lamb. Lamb of God.
jokes.setup: Marge apologizes for calling her the wrong name
jokes.punchline: Agnes's over-the-top religious explanation of her name
jokes.explanation: Perfect Agnes moment - petty and self-important

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: I'm so sorry for calling you the wrong name.

dialogue.speaker: Agnes
dialogue.line: Well, my name is Agnes, named after Saint Agnes, the patron saint of chastity. She was a young Christian martyr who was executed for refusing to marry a pagan Roman. Her name comes from the Latin 'agnus,' meaning lamb, symbolizing her purity and innocence. So when you call me by the wrong name, you're not just disrespecting me—you're disrespecting centuries of Christian tradition and the very concept of virginal martyrdom!

jokes.text: If these trends continue- 'Ey!
jokes.setup: Disco Stu promoting disco with 1976 statistics
jokes.punchline: Cut off mid-sentence, highlighting how outdated he is
jokes.explanation: Classic Disco Stu obliviousness to being decades behind

dialogue.speaker: Disco Stu
dialogue.line: Hey there, baby! Disco is alive and well! Look at these groovy 1976 statistics - 73% of Americans love the hustle, bell-bottoms are flying off the shelves, and the Bee Gees are—

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Uh, Disco Stu? It's 2023.

dialogue.speaker: Disco Stu
dialogue.line: What? No, man, you're—

jokes.text: Yeah, I know. I can't get them out of there.
jokes.setup: Someone points out his fish are dead
jokes.punchline: Casual acceptance of dead fish in his setup
jokes.explanation: Disco Stu's incompetence delivered matter-of-factly

dialogue.speaker: Disco Stu
dialogue.line: Oh yeah, those guys have been floating around for like three weeks. I just figured they were doing some kind of new dance.

jokes.text: Well, I don't know about food from the Middle East. Isn't that whole area a little iffy? [Laughs] Hey, I'm no geographer.
jokes.setup: Discussing Middle Eastern food
jokes.punchline: Casual ignorance followed by admission he doesn't know geography
jokes.explanation: Typical ignorant American salesman trope

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Middle Eastern food? Sure, I love that stuff. Falafel, hummus, those little meat on a stick things...

dialogue.speaker: Franchise Salesman
dialogue.line: Actually, sir, those are Greek.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Greek, Middle Eastern—what's the difference? They're all right next to each other, right?

dialogue.speaker: Franchise Salesman
dialogue.line: Well, geographically speaking, they're quite—

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Look, I don't know geography, okay? I thought the Middle East was in the middle of the East.

jokes.text: You and I, why don't we call it pocket bread? Huh?
jokes.setup: Uncomfortable with foreign food terms
jokes.punchline: Suggests Americanizing pita bread name
jokes.explanation: Cultural insensitivity played for mild humor

dialogue.speaker: Homer Simpson
dialogue.line: What's a pita?

dialogue.speaker: Franchise Salesman
dialogue.line: It's a Mediterranean flatbread.

dialogue.speaker: Homer Simpson
dialogue.line: Mediterranean? That's too fancy. Why can't we just call it a round bread?

dialogue.speaker: Franchise Salesman
dialogue.line: Because it's called a pita.

dialogue.speaker: Homer Simpson
dialogue.line: How about we call it a... Freedom Pocket?

jokes.text: - Uh, what's tahini? - Flavor sauce. - And falafel? - Crunch patties.
jokes.setup: Marge asking about Middle Eastern foods
jokes.punchline: Salesman's ridiculous American translations
jokes.explanation: Escalating absurdity in food renaming, good economy

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Do you have any Middle Eastern foods?

dialogue.speaker: Franchise Salesman
dialogue.line: We have hummus.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Hummus?

dialogue.speaker: Franchise Salesman
dialogue.line: Yeah, it's chickpea paste. We call it 'Garbanzo Bean Goop.'

dialogue.speaker: Franchise Salesman
dialogue.line: And we have falafel, which we call 'Fried Chick Pea Balls.'

dialogue.speaker: Franchise Salesman
dialogue.line: And tabbouleh, which we call 'Lettuce Surprise.'

jokes.text: Here. Try a Ben Franklin.
jokes.setup: Offering food to try
jokes.punchline: Names Middle Eastern food after American historical figure
jokes.explanation: Continues the Americanization theme with historical reference

dialogue.speaker: Franchise Salesman
dialogue.line: Try our new sandwich! We call it the Lincoln.

dialogue.speaker: Customer
dialogue.line: Why is it called the Lincoln?

dialogue.speaker: Franchise Salesman
dialogue.line: Because it's got four score and seven layers!

jokes.text: Tabouleh and rezmi-kabob. Uh, that's our chef, Christopher.
jokes.setup: Explaining what's in the 'Ben Franklin'
jokes.punchline: Chef with generic American name making Middle Eastern food
jokes.explanation: Continues cultural appropriation theme

dialogue.speaker: Franchise Salesman
dialogue.line: The 'Ben Franklin' features our most popular item: a Middle Eastern scramble, prepared by our chef, Chet Englebrett.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Chet Englebrett?

dialogue.speaker: Franchise Salesman
dialogue.line: It's a very American name.

jokes.text: Then God tossed me a life preserver- a tasty, golden-brown life preserver.
jokes.setup: Frank describing his salvation
jokes.punchline: Life preserver metaphor for pretzels
jokes.explanation: Nice metaphor connecting pretzel shape to life preserver

dialogue.speaker: Frank Ormand
dialogue.line: I was drowning in my own despair, until I found my life preserver.

dialogue.speaker: Frank Ormand
dialogue.line: Pretzels.

jokes.text: 'Knot' bread. You get it? Huh?
jokes.setup: It's not only not bad, it's not bread
jokes.punchline: Pun on 'knot' shape of pretzels vs 'not'
jokes.explanation: Classic groan-worthy pun that fits the salesman character

dialogue.speaker: Frank Ormand
dialogue.line: It's not only not bad, it's not bread

jokes.text: Edna! Bart!
jokes.setup: Maude excitedly listing franchise members
jokes.punchline: Includes Bart, a child, as equal member
jokes.explanation: Absurd inclusion of child in business venture

dialogue.speaker: Maude
dialogue.line: Well, we have Homer, Marge, Lisa, Maggie, and Bart as equal members of the franchise!

jokes.text: Wherever a young mother is ignorant of what to feed her baby... you'll be there.
jokes.setup: Describing pretzel business territory
jokes.punchline: Targeting vulnerable new mothers with junk food
jokes.explanation: Dark humor about exploiting parental uncertainty

dialogue.speaker: Frank Ormand
dialogue.line: We target vulnerable new mothers with junk food.

jokes.text: Wherever nacho penetration is less than total, you'll be there.
jokes.setup: Continuing territorial description
jokes.punchline: Military language applied to snack food distribution
jokes.explanation: Absurd militaristic language for snack food marketing

dialogue.speaker: Frank Ormand
dialogue.line: We have a very strict territorial system here. The Doritos are ours, the Cheetos belong to the guys on the other side of the fence.

dialogue.speaker: Frank Ormand
dialogue.line: If they try to advance into our snack zone, we launch a full-scale assault with Funyuns.

jokes.text: Wherever a Bavarian is not quite full, you will be there.
jokes.setup: Continuing the territorial speech
jokes.punchline: Targeting specific ethnic group for pretzel sales
jokes.explanation: Logical connection to pretzels being German, absurd specificity

jokes.text: Don't forget fat people. They can't stop eating.
jokes.setup: Adding to target market description
jokes.punchline: Blunt exploitation of eating disorders
jokes.explanation: Dark but somewhat cheap humor targeting obesity

jokes.text: And not the kind that attacked my intestinal lining some years back.
jokes.setup: Frank mentioning pretzel fever
jokes.punchline: TMI about his medical condition
jokes.explanation: Classic inappropriate oversharing in instructional video

dialogue.speaker: Frank Ormand
dialogue.line: I got pretzel fever. It's a serious condition. First, I crave pretzels. Then I think of bacon. Then I get all queasy.

jokes.text: An automatic garage door opener makes you feel like... you're working in a futuristic wonder world.
jokes.setup: Frank describing office setup
jokes.punchline: Overenthusiastic description of basic technology
jokes.explanation: Frank's delusional optimism about mundane things

dialogue.speaker: Frank Grimes
dialogue.line: Look at this — a state-of-the-art workstation! Ergonomic chair, dual monitors, high-speed internet connection. I've never seen anything like it! And the coffee machine — it's got a timer! A TIMER!

jokes.text: A phony ticker-tape parade will help you avoid littering laws.
jokes.setup: Suggesting how to distribute flyers
jokes.punchline: Elaborate scheme to circumvent littering laws
jokes.explanation: Brilliantly absurd legal loophole suggestion

dialogue.speaker: Frank Ormand
dialogue.line: We'll attach the flyers to small helium balloons and release them into the atmosphere. When they deflate and fall, they'll technically be litter, but we'll have already moved on!

jokes.text: Check for millipedes. [Groans]
jokes.setup: Frank instructing how to check ingredients
jokes.punchline: Marge finds actual millipedes in the pretzel mix
jokes.explanation: Gross-out humor that reveals poor quality of franchise

dialogue.speaker: Frank Ormand
dialogue.line: Always check the ingredients carefully. Look for any foreign objects.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Oh my God! There are actual millipedes in here!

jokes.text: Why, it's one of those pretzel wagons the movie stars are always talking about.
jokes.setup: Worker noticing Marge's pretzel wagon
jokes.punchline: Sarcastic reference to non-existent movie star endorsements
jokes.explanation: Mocks the pretzel business's false advertising claims

dialogue.speaker: Worker
dialogue.line: Hey, isn't that Marge Simpson's pretzel wagon? I didn't know she had a celebrity endorsement deal.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: I don't have a celebrity endorsement.

dialogue.speaker: Worker
dialogue.line: Oh, my mistake. I could have sworn I saw Brad Pitt eating one of your pretzels on TV.

jokes.text: Uh, let's see. I'll have one, uh- [Mutters] Hey, hurry up. I wanna get my pretzel.
jokes.setup: Customer taking forever to order simple pretzel
jokes.punchline: Another customer complaining about the wait for such a simple order
jokes.explanation: Standard slow service humor

dialogue.speaker: Customer 1
dialogue.line: I'll have a pretzel.

dialogue.speaker: Vendor
dialogue.line: One pretzel coming up!

dialogue.speaker: Customer 2
dialogue.line: Come on! How long does it take to get a pretzel?!

jokes.text: Wow! Check out that van. It looks like it doesn't even need our business!
jokes.setup: Customers see Fleet-A-Pita van
jokes.punchline: Van's appearance suggests financial success and independence
jokes.explanation: Good observation about how appearance affects customer perception

dialogue.speaker: Customer 1
dialogue.line: Look at that Fleet-A-Pita van! Business must be booming!

dialogue.speaker: Customer 2
dialogue.line: Yeah, he's really made it. Total independence.

jokes.text: Gimbel's is gone, Marge. Long gone. You're Gimbel's.
jokes.setup: Marge suggests they can coexist like Macy's and Gimbel's
jokes.punchline: Helen crushing Marge's optimism by pointing out Gimbel's failed
jokes.explanation: Excellent dark humor with perfect economy - Helen's cruelty delivered with devastating precision

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: We can coexist like Macy's and Gimbel's.

dialogue.speaker: Helen
dialogue.line: Gimbel's went out of business.

jokes.text: Hey, I don't think so. I got me 300 coupons.
jokes.setup: Marge quotes $300 for 300 pretzels
jokes.punchline: Cletus reveals he has coupons for all of them
jokes.explanation: Good setup/payoff showing Marge's business naivety

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: That'll be $300 for 300 pretzels.

dialogue.speaker: Cletus
dialogue.line: Well, hot diggity! I got coupons for all of 'em!

jokes.text: Shoulda but didna, so hand 'em over.
jokes.setup: Marge lamenting not putting limits on coupons
jokes.punchline: Cletus's folksy grammar and ruthless business sense
jokes.explanation: Perfect Cletus characterization - folksy but cunning

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: I should have put a limit on those coupons.

dialogue.speaker: Cletus
dialogue.line: Well, I ain't complainin'. I done bought me a lifetime supply of turtle wax and I ain't even got a car.

jokes.text: Come on. Tiffany, Heather, Cody, Dillon... Dermott, Jordan, Taylor, Brittany... Wesley, Rumor, Scout, Cassidy... Zoe, Chloe, Max, Hunter... Kendall, Caitlan, Noah, Sasha... Morgan, Kira, Ian, Lauren... Q-Bert, Phil. [Chuckles]
jokes.setup: Cletus calling his many children
jokes.punchline: Long list of trendy names ending with video game character Q-Bert
jokes.explanation: Brilliant escalation from trendy names to absurd video game reference, becomes funnier with repetition

dialogue.speaker: Cletus
dialogue.line: Come on, kids! Dinner time! Brandon! Crystal! Tyler! Amber! Mestiza! Incontinentia! Q-Bert!

jokes.text: 'Copyright 1968'? Hmm. Determined or not, that cat must be long dead. That's kind of a downer.
jokes.setup: Marge looking at motivational cat poster
jokes.punchline: Realizing the cat is probably dead due to poster's age
jokes.explanation: Brilliant dark turn on inspirational posters, perfectly captures Marge's practical mindset

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Hang in there... Oh my, this poster must be thirty years old. The cat is probably dead.

jokes.text: Montgomery Burns. - [Crowd Boos] - Yea! Yea!
jokes.setup: Random drawing for car giveaway
jokes.punchline: Rich Burns wins while crowd boos, but he celebrates
jokes.explanation: Perfect Burns moment - already rich but still greedy for more

dialogue.speaker: Announcer
dialogue.line: And the winner of our new car is... Mr. Burns!

dialogue.speaker: Crowd
dialogue.line: Booooo!

dialogue.speaker: Burns
dialogue.line: Excellent! A new car! And the crowd is chanting my name!

jokes.text: And here come the pretzels. Oh, no! Oh, don't do that! You're supposed to be tasting them!
jokes.setup: Crowd angry about Burns winning
jokes.punchline: Crowd throws Marge's pretzels while she protests their misuse
jokes.explanation: Great escalation with Marge's misplaced priorities during chaos

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Those are for eating, not throwing!

dialogue.speaker: Announcer
dialogue.line: The crowd has spoken!

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: But my pretzels!

jokes.text: Hall of Famer Whitey Ford now on the field... pleading with the crowd for- for some kind of sanity. Uh-oh. And a barrage of pretzels... now knocking Whitey unconscious. Wow. This is a-This is a black day for baseball.
jokes.setup: Baseball legend tries to calm pretzel-throwing crowd
jokes.punchline: Pretzels knock out the Hall of Famer, announcer's grave assessment
jokes.explanation: Excellent escalation from protest to assault on baseball legend, announcer's serious tone makes it funnier

dialogue.speaker: Baseball Legend
dialogue.line: Hey, hey, everybody, calm down! There's no need to throw pretzels!

dialogue.speaker: Crowd
dialogue.line: *throws pretzels*

dialogue.speaker: Announcer
dialogue.line: Oh my God, he's been hit! He's down! He's... he's not getting up. Ladies and gentlemen, we may have just witnessed a death.

jokes.text: You could call them 'Whitey Whackers.'
jokes.setup: Lisa trying to cheer up Marge about the incident
jokes.punchline: Suggesting brand name based on injuring celebrity
jokes.explanation: Dark humor from smart Lisa, good alliteration

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Mom, look on the bright side. You could start your own line of airbags. Call them 'The Marge Simpson Airbag System.' They work great—I can vouch for that personally.

jokes.text: Listen to your mother, kids. Aim low. Aim so low, no one will even care if you succeed.
jokes.setup: Marge feeling defeated about her business
jokes.punchline: Giving children terrible life advice in depression
jokes.explanation: Brilliant dark comedy - Marge's depression leading to hilariously bad parenting advice

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Well, kids, I've learned that if you want something in life, you just have to give up.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Don't even try. It's easier that way.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: And remember, whenever life gets you down, just lie in bed and cry until you fall asleep.

jokes.text: Dinner's in the oven. You want some butter, it's under my face.
jokes.setup: Marge completely defeated, lying face-down
jokes.punchline: Matter-of-fact practical instruction while prostrate with depression
jokes.explanation: Perfect blend of physical comedy and character - Marge's depression expressed through domestic duty

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: If anyone needs me, I'll be face-down in the dirt.

jokes.text: Marge needs help. And God knows I'm not the man to provide it. But I know who can.
jokes.setup: Homer recognizing Marge needs help
jokes.punchline: Homer's rare moment of self-awareness about his limitations
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Homer's occasional self-awareness

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Marge needs help, and I'm just gonna mess it up. I always do. Maybe I should call someone who's actually competent.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Nah, who am I kidding? I'm gonna help anyway and everything will be terrible.

jokes.text: They were in the same car.
jokes.setup: Homer asks to speak to Frank Ormand's estate executor
jokes.punchline: Reveals executor also died in same accident
jokes.explanation: Dark humor delivered matter-of-factly

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: I'd like to speak to Frank Ormand's estate executor.

dialogue.speaker: Funeral Director
dialogue.line: I'm sorry, he also died in the same accident.

jokes.text: See, boss? I told ya that ad in the church bulletin would pay off.
jokes.setup: Fat Tony agrees to help Homer after prayer
jokes.punchline: Mobster treating church as business networking opportunity
jokes.explanation: Clever subversion of religious setting with mob business

dialogue.speaker: Fat Tony
dialogue.line: Homer, I'm gonna help you out. But first, let me work the room a little bit.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Uh, sure, Fat Tony.

dialogue.speaker: Fat Tony
dialogue.line: Hey, Bishop! How's business? We should do lunch sometime. I know a great place—it's got a wine cellar in the basement, if you know what I mean.

jokes.text: I believe it was a 'boaking' accident.
jokes.setup: Man claims injured fingers from boating accident
jokes.punchline: Fat Tony correcting to 'boaking' (boat + breaking)
jokes.explanation: Clever mob euphemism for violence

dialogue.speaker: Fat Tony
dialogue.line: No, no, no. It's not a 'boating' accident. It's a 'boaking' accident. The boat... it broke.

jokes.text: Look, lady, if I was you... I would just leap into the air... as I'm preparing to do.
jokes.setup: Woman asking about men under her van
jokes.punchline: Worker's bizarre advice to jump while demonstrating
jokes.explanation: Absurd advice delivered with complete conviction

dialogue.speaker: Woman
dialogue.line: There's a man under my van!

dialogue.speaker: Dock Worker
dialogue.line: Well, jump up and down on it.

dialogue.speaker: Woman
dialogue.line: What?

dialogue.speaker: Dock Worker
dialogue.line: Jump up and down on it. That'll scare 'em out.

dialogue.speaker: Dock Worker
dialogue.line: See? Like this.

jokes.text: Hey, wait a minute. This is real money.
jokes.setup: Moe saying Homer's money is no good
jokes.punchline: Surprise that Homer actually has legitimate money
jokes.explanation: Good character moment for Moe - surprised by actual payment. Implies Homer usually doesn't pay

dialogue.speaker: Moe
dialogue.line: Your money's no good here, Homer.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: What? Why not?

dialogue.speaker: Moe
dialogue.line: Because it's... it's real money.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Oh. Well, that's just great.

jokes.text: You mean the mob only did me a favor to get something in return? Oh, Fat Tony! I will say good day to you, sir.
jokes.setup: Fat Tony explains favor system
jokes.punchline: Homer's naive shock and overly formal dismissal
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer innocence and formal indignation

dialogue.speaker: Fat Tony
dialogue.line: You see, Homer, in our thing, when somebody does you a favor, you owe them a favor back. It's how it works.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Wait, so if you do me a favor, I have to do you a favor?

dialogue.speaker: Fat Tony
dialogue.line: Exactly.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Well, I'm afraid I must respectfully decline your entire operation!

jokes.text: Oh. You have to push in as you turn. - Ah. - Yeah, that's it.
jokes.setup: Marge kidnapped and having trouble with handcuffs
jokes.punchline: Polite instruction from captor on how to use restraints
jokes.explanation: Brilliantly absurd politeness during kidnapping, very Simpsons

dialogue.speaker: Mobster
dialogue.line: You gotta push the little button on the side.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Oh!

dialogue.speaker: Mobster
dialogue.line: No, the other side.

jokes.text: And to show you we're serious, you have 12 hours.
jokes.setup: Fat Tony gives 24-hour deadline
jokes.punchline: Immediately reduces it to 12 hours for emphasis
jokes.explanation: Good mob logic - making threats more threatening by being inconsistent

dialogue.speaker: Fat Tony
dialogue.line: You got 24 hours to pay up.

dialogue.speaker: Fat Tony
dialogue.line: Actually, make that 12 hours. I want to emphasize the urgency.

jokes.text: In those words? Yes.
jokes.setup: Marge asks if Homer told mob to eliminate competitors with violence
jokes.punchline: Homer's matter-of-fact confirmation
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer moment - complete lack of remorse or understanding

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Homer, did you tell the mob to eliminate your competitors with violence?

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Yes.

jokes.text: I saw you desperately trying to cram one more salty treat... into America's already-bloated snack hole.
jokes.setup: Homer explaining why he contacted the mob
jokes.punchline: Surprisingly articulate description of snack food market saturation
jokes.explanation: Brilliant Homer observation with unexpectedly sophisticated vocabulary

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Well, you see, the snack food market has become increasingly saturated with competitors, each vying for shelf space and consumer attention. The traditional players like Lay's and Doritos have faced pressure from artisanal and health-conscious alternatives, fragmenting the demographic. Meanwhile, convenience stores have limited inventory, forcing distributors to make tough choices about which brands to stock. It's basic supply and demand economics, really.

jokes.text: I reached out to some violent mobsters.
jokes.setup: Homer explaining his loving husband actions
jokes.punchline: Casual mention of contacting violent criminals
jokes.explanation: Homer's twisted logic delivered matter-of-factly

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: I'm a loving husband. I shower Marge with affection, I buy her flowers, I even know her favorite color.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Of course, I had to call some violent criminals to find that last one out, but still.

jokes.text: I had that dream again!
jokes.setup: Loud knocking creates suspense about mob arrival
jokes.punchline: Just Grampa with non-sequitur about recurring dream
jokes.explanation: Good tension relief with classic Grampa randomness

dialogue.speaker: Grampa
dialogue.line: I keep having this dream where I'm chasing a blue whale through the streets of Shelbyville, and it keeps saying 'Grampa, why won't you love me?' and I say 'Because you're a whale!'

jokes.text: 'Where's the money? When are you going to get the money? Why aren't you getting the money now?' And so on. So, please, da money.
jokes.setup: Fat Tony explaining his wife's pressure
jokes.punchline: Mob boss henpecked like regular husband
jokes.explanation: Humanizes mob boss with relatable domestic pressure

dialogue.speaker: Fat Tony
dialogue.line: My wife, she's always on me. 'Tony, why you never home? Tony, why you always with the guys?' It's like I'm some kind of regular husband or something.

jokes.text: Where's the money? When are you going to get the money? Why aren't you getting the money now?
jokes.setup: Fat Tony explaining wife's nagging
jokes.punchline: Mob boss dealing with domestic pressure about money collection
jokes.explanation: Good humanizing of mob boss - even criminals have nagging spouses. Relatable domestic pressure

dialogue.speaker: Fat Tony
dialogue.line: My wife, she's always on my case about the money. 'Fat Tony, where's the collections?' 'Fat Tony, why you come home so late?' 'Fat Tony, why you got bullet holes in your shirt?'

dialogue.speaker: Fat Tony
dialogue.line: I tell her, 'Sweetheart, you think it's easy running a criminal empire? You think the guys respect you when your wife's yelling at you in front of them?'

jokes.text: TheJapanese Mafia? They'll kill ya five times before ya hit the ground.
jokes.setup: Helen introducing Yakuza
jokes.punchline: Impossible mathematical claim about killing speed
jokes.explanation: Absurd exaggeration of martial arts mythology

jokes.text: But, Marge, that little guy hasn't done anything yet. Look at him. He's gonna do something... and you know it's gonna be good.
jokes.setup: Marge wants to go inside during mob fight
jokes.punchline: Homer excited to see smallest fighter's contribution
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer priorities - violence as entertainment

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Homer, we have to get inside!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Wait, wait. I want to see the little guy's contribution.

jokes.text: Oh, I don't hate you for failing. I love you for trying.
jokes.setup: Homer thinks Marge hates him for his failures
jokes.punchline: Marge's touching but also cutting response
jokes.explanation: Perfect Marge line - sweet but with underlying resignation about Homer's competence

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Homer, I don't hate you. I could never hate you. But I am disappointed. Disappointed that you haven't achieved your potential. Disappointed that you keep making the same mistakes. But most of all, I'm disappointed that you think I would hate you for being human.

jokes.text: Oh, it's just a mob war. Go back to sleep, honey.
jokes.setup: Bart asks what's happening outside
jokes.punchline: Marge casually dismissing violent mob conflict
jokes.explanation: Marge's matter-of-fact reaction to extreme violence outside

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: What's happening outside?

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Oh, just some people with pitchforks and torches. Nothing to worry about.

tmdb_overview: Marge's new pretzel franchise is failing until Homer strikes a deal with Fat Tony, Springfield's local mobster.
insight: Marge's business venture generates 68 jokes in 22 minutes, mostly character-driven absurdism.
